description: A dropped cup comes apart, or a spring loses the wholeness that let it work. 'pukate' names that complete break. 'pukeri' (damage) covers a worsening condition or function when the thing may still remain whole.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and release them into high-back rounded 'u'. Press the back of the tongue against the soft palate for 'k', release it into stressed open central 'a', then place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for dental 't' and finish with middle-height front 'e'.
sound_symbolism: With closures at the lips, soft palate, and teeth, the mouth keeps stopping and starting. The word itself arrives in pieces.
usage_notes: The thing that breaks is the subject at base. Causative 'ka' adds the person or force that breaks it, and the event-noun rule supplies a break.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Repair begins by noticing whether a thing is worn, damaged, or fully broken. 'pukate' keeps the last condition available without treating disposal as its necessary result.

semantic_domains.activity: an event in which a whole loses the structure or continuity that held it together
semantic_domains.physical: complete loss of wholeness in an object or material
